speaker: interviewer
text: --five. We're interviewing Ernest Rubin. It's March 19, 1995. We're in Brooklyn, New York. The interviewer is Ricky [? Halperin, ?] and the interview is in English.

speaker: interviewee
text: I can continue? So I end up in Somotor. Finally. You know. In the morning, I remember, my mother said if-- for this lady-- [? Teresa ?] [? Biwies ?] is her name. She was our neighbor. And if somebody survives, [? Teresa, ?] please, take care of my children, my mother told her.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I'm coming down on the street in the morning, you know, in my German uniform, and I recognize this woman with her neighbor talking. And I'm coming close to her. And she's saying to the other woman, look at this, a German soldier. So I come closer to her, and I say, hello. I say, do you know who I am? She said, no. I said, I'm Hershel. Oh, my god. She goes to my neck. She kisses me, hugs me. She puts me in bed. For three months, she was feeding me by spoon. You know? She washed me, you know, like-- like a mother. She went on the farm to work. You know, she had the field. She left me there in the bed the whole day to lay. And all of a sudden, someone is knocking on the door one--a few days later, and they bring a little piece of paper. Somebody threw it down from the train. And a boy brings it over. They knew my name was Hershel. And here I'm reading the paper-- I see Zippora arrived in-- in Budapest. So what happened? I had no clothing. See, I took her son's clothing, some old pants and a jacket, barefoot. And I run out from the house. She didn't know what happened to me. And I see a train coming, because it slowed down there near the train station. I jump on the freight train into Hungary, cross the border into Budapest. In Budapest, we met my sister, and together, we came home. So I knew already that my brother is home, Jacob, because she told me he's in jail. What happened? This family, Rabovsky, helped us so much before the concentration camp. They killed the whole family-- children, daughter, son, everybody was wiped out. And the son-in-law was alive who was an anti-Semite who said only the Jews could do it to pay back for us. And finally, I went there to [INAUDIBLE] with this lady, [? Teresa. ?] And he said they-- at 11:30, they bring him into jail to take him out of work. And I don't say nothing. I come with her on the sidewalk in the city of [INAUDIBLE]. And I say, see, that's-- that's [INAUDIBLE]. I recognize them. I said, don't say nothing. I said come close. She say, hello, [? Teresa. ?] He's greeting her [? Teresa, ?] you know, [INAUDIBLE]. And she said, don't you know who it is? I said no, who's this? I said, don't you know your brother Hershel? He said, I didn't see him for four years. So he recognized me. Of course, we start crying.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the-- so I started to work. They should let them out from jail. Finally, I managed to-- you know, because the organization was not there yet. So there was one Jewish lady who was very rich. She gave bail, she put down bail. And after three months, they let them out of jail, because apparently, the-- the people who killed-- the person who killed this Rabovsky family killed a baron in the next town. It was the Russians. They robbed the own house, and that's-- that's the way it ended. And then, you know, of course, came the black marketing, you know, again to survive, to build up the house.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long were you in Somotor?

speaker: interviewee
text: Somotor. Till 1948, May.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then what happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: Then I joined Haganah, and we went into the Czech Army. They created the Czechoslovakian brigade, all Jewish boys and girls. And they trained us in the Czechoslovakia army for six months. And then after six months, they send us over to Israel to-- with a special train to Romania, Romania boat to Israel. And I was in the army there for two years. And after the army, I came out, I couldn't find jobs. It was very bad. You know. And-- for those days, it was--

speaker: interviewer
text: So you remained in Israel until when?

speaker: interviewee
text: Four years. I stayed two years in the army, two years-- you know, and like I said, after four years, I couldn't make it. And my cousin from Canada sent me some papers to come to Canada, and the papers-- the-- the permit to go to Canada was expiring in two weeks. And they closed down the immigration. So the-- I was supposed to go to England to meet-- to have a medical examination, because there was no Canadian consulate in Israel those days yet.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I figured I've got to go to France. The French let me in. They gave me a visa. And I figured that they can send over the papers to Paris, and that's where I'm gonna go through the medical examination. I would go through. But it didn't work out like this. The Canadian consulate said, we don't see no reason-- I had the letter already-- why you shouldn't come to England. Go tell them a story that the British don't let me in. I have to wait six month. So that's what happened. I went in Paris to the-- to the English consulate to ask for a visa. And they said, OK, we gonna give you the visa, but you have to wait six months. In the meantime, my paper expired, and I get stuck in Paris. I had to renew the papers. I didn't want to renew it, because they say who ask you to go to Paris? You should have stayed there, wait till they call you.

speaker: interviewee
text: And in the meantime, I have no money. I have $10 in my pocket and $155 in my shoe, because it was illegal to take out the money from Israel. So $10. I arrive at the boat in Marseilles. Right away-- I went with other Jews. There are other Jews, refugees. And right away, I spent 1,500 francs for a-- for a room in the hotel. So I had leftover only $7, 500 francs-- dollar. Then I go-- I had an address, this guy who was saved in Hungary. His-- his name is Oscar [? Lazinga. ?] They took me into the house. I became a maid. It didn't matter for me, because I had no money. And he said, the only thing you will do, you will take the two children in the park, and you're gonna clean the house. I do anything just for a roof, because I had no money, and the food was not the best.

speaker: interviewer
text: In the-- in the meantime, also, before they took me in, I met another Israeli boy who was sleeping for 100 francs, $0.20 in a hotel a night. So I said, if you want, you give me $0.10, we can sleep in one bed. You pay half of the bed. So $0.10, you know, a dollar was going far. So then finally, I managed to renew the papers. And in the meantime, I met my wife, I needed her. Because every 48 hours, they kicked me out from France. Because in the passport was written that I came to France for business profession, laborer. Already, it's not kosher. You understand? So from France--

speaker: interviewee
text: She came constantly, every week, to the police to extend my visa. Finally, in the day she was going away with her parents to-- [INAUDIBLE] you know, for vacation, I stay there alone in the apartment, because the-- the wife and the children went away for vacation. And all of a sudden, the day when she leaves for vacation, I'm alone there, because the-- the husband goes to the business, the butcher store, [? salami factory ?] there and everything. And all of a sudden, someone knocks on the door. The lady, the super, comes in. She brings in the mail. I look. The visa arrived from England. They called me to get the visa. So that's the way I end up after-- in [INAUDIBLE], the same place where she was on vacation. And I sent a-- somebody helped me to send a telegram. And she had to run to the boat, because it takes about three hours to cross the channel. And I'm waiting, waiting for her. And I couldn't speak English. I asked the captain-- my fiance, fiance. You know, he didn't understand, but finally understood. He made the boat wait an extra five or six minute. You could put in a movie. And they are calling her name. Her name was Gorgette Meslin. And all of a sudden, I say, captain says, I'm sorry. The boat has to leave. The boat is leaving. All of a sudden, I see she's running out to the boat. And she sees me that I'm waving, and she collapsed there. And everybody in the boat-- you know, like they were shooting a movie, everybody is clapping, you know, like [LAUGHS] I would be a celebrity or something. And then I end up in England-- in England two weeks. I was in a shelter there. They gave me a bed but no food. So I managed to push through there already. And I get my visa. I end up in Canada. And in Canada, I was in Montreal for a year and a half. And I was-- work, it was very hard. And wherever I went--

speaker: interviewer
text: So you got married in Canada?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, after-- nine months after. She-- my wife came after me. She converted to Judaism. And-- and then we get married. And luckily, [? Steinbeck, ?] the supermarket, was anybody who get married in May got a $10 packet food. So it was OK. And my friend, who was in Florida, gave me a $10 present. Because I remember he told me he had $630 already saved up. And with this, finally, but we couldn't make it there. And my wife, she was going to work. They needed a sales lady, and she had experience. She goes there. She registered. They ask what religion you are. She says she's Jewish. She just turned Jewish. They said, I'm sorry. We hire only Catholics.

speaker: interviewer
text: She comes home crying. I said you're a Jew only a few days, already you have the experience? What shall I say? That we-- for 2,000 years, we are suffering. So the next day, another department store looking for sales ladies. She goes there. Don't put there-- down that you are Jewish. Put down you are Catholic. I'm not lying to you. Simpsons is the name for the-- for the department store. They hire only Protestant. So then I decided-- you know, because it didn't go-- I make a joke from it. Wherever I went, my boss died, had a heart attack, or the building burned down, or the company went bankrupt. I was so unlucky that my friends used to make a joke with me and say, Hershel, move away from Montreal, because the whole city will be destroyed because of you. So I end up in Toronto. In Toronto, I became a painter. I learn painting a little bit. And then, of course, already I become independent. And in no-- When did you arrive to the United States?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Untied States, I arrived in 1962 in July.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about your family here. How many children do you have?

speaker: interviewee
text: I have two children, excuse me, a son and a daughter. My daughter has two little girls, and my son has two boys and a little girl.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what has been your occupation in the United States?

speaker: interviewee
text: United States, I was painting for nine months by myself. I couldn't get help, because it was terrible here. I get one guy, he get drunk. He was drinking shellac. I don't know if you know what shellac is. I left him there. I come back, he is dead drunk. You know. So I get scared. I had no insurance composition. So I told him, I call you next morning. I brought coffee from the [? luncheonette. ?] I woke him up. I said, don't worry, boss. I'm all right. I do it always. He separated the alcohol from the shellac. I don't know if you know what shellac is. And after this, I decided I have sold my property in Canada. And I bought a taxi right away, and I bought a house right away. And I had an apartment for two years. And she was pregnant. And my daughter was born already here in this house, in-- in the new house. And I do the taxi since then. But first, I went bankrupt. I lost all they money, because the builder made a phony bankruptcy in Canada. And I had three houses, and I had about 20 men working for me. And all my money, I was paying the labor. And I lost all my money. But I managed to have the three houses. I-- exactly, I pulled out from the houses $22,000. I put a down payment on a cab, $11,000, and down payment on a house. And just when the-- the contract for the-- the lease was finished there, I moved into the new house, and I was taxing already. And since then, I'm in  the taxi business.

words.text: I don't know if you know

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Now we're going to look at some photographs.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. OK. This is my mother. [POINTS] I found it when I came back from the concentration camp on the field, and I made a copy for everybody, for my sister and for my brother. This is--

speaker: interviewer
text: When do you think this was taken?

words.text: do you think this was taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is probably before she get married, I think. She was maybe already engaged.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who's that one in the corner?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's-- that's me.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is [POINTS] a picture also I found on the field when I came home from the concentration-- I walking around there, and I found this. [POINTING] And this is my mother, rest in peace. This is my big sister, who was married in [INAUDIBLE] had three children. And one of her children was in our house.

speaker: interviewer
text: This is-- also, I think she was engaged. It must be around-- because I must be about five years old here. Maybe in '34, 1934? And this is my sister, Zippora who lives in [? Boro ?] Park. And this is my brother, Betzalel, who died, unfortunately, Melk. He was about two years [POINTING] older than me. And this is my little sister Toby, who was eight or nine year old. And who-- remember I told you in Auschwitz, she died there. Show us where you are again, please. Where you are in the picture.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, [POINT] that's me. I forgot that I am there also. [LAUGHS] OK. This is our license-- the liquor license. Because you couldn't get a liquor license. You had to go by generation from the family to family. If you can see, if you point the camera there, in 1804. I don't know if you can see that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah?

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm.

speaker: interviewee
text: And this also I found on the field, you know, when I came back with the few pictures. Every four years, I assume, had to be renewed. Because I see 1804, 1808, and 1812 there somewhere. OK. This is me [POINTS] when I arrived in Israel from Czechoslovakia with a brigade in-- in the Israeli Army.

speaker: interviewer
text: In what year?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is the-- [POINTS] This is early 1949. I arrived the end of '48 to Israel. But I-- we came already as soldiers straight to the army camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were trained in Czechoslovakia for the Israeli army?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. It was Czechoslovakian brigade volunteers who went to fight for Israel. This-- this is our wedding picture. I was very, very rich. My friend, he was a photographer. I didn't have a penny in my pocket, so everybody was helping. He made the pictures for free. Because I have to tell you about the rabbi who gave us the-- make the wedding-- who converted my wife to Judaism. He wanted $250 for the conversion. And I didn't have the money. When my wife arrived from France, she had exactly $250. Her mother sold the piano. And she was working for [INAUDIBLE] Lafayette. I don't know. There was a department store on 57th Street, [INAUDIBLE] Lafayette. She was working there between school, and she economized the $250. And I asked the rabbi, give me back $5. I should be able to have a sandwich for my wedding night. He wouldn't give me the $5. So this I will take my grave, and I will never forgive him this. OK. This is a picture of our neighbor. He's the middle [POINTS] man. He-- her-- his mother took me in for three months. She was taking care of me and bathed me and feed me until I get back on my feet. And this is his wife. This is me. We went back a couple years ago to Czechoslovakia. So I went. I filled up the car in France from food to cognac to clothing, everything, and I gave it to them. And this is the guy without a shirt. He's the one on the end who brought me the food into the ghetto, and he threw it over the gate in order we should have the three weeks what we eat.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is their name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mejarosz Gesa. This is the house where I was born. And that's the way it looks now. It-- it was different when they took us to the concentration camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the address of the house?

speaker: interviewee
text: 123 Somotor. There's the-- if-- if you can it there, the sign. 123 is the house number. Oh, this is [POINTS] my sister on the end. In the middle, it's my brother. Unfortunately, he died two months ago. And this is me. They came here. I think it was the bris for one of my grandchildren [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: So that's Zippora?

speaker: interviewee
text: Zippora Jacob, and that's me.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. This is the cemetery in [INAUDIBLE] where my mother-- apparently, there are 19 or 25 people buried in the same grave. My mother, she died in the train when they took her to Auschwitz about a month later when they took me. How I found out that she died-- the doctor who was our family doctor-- his name was Dr. Tziner. Remember, I mentioned that his brother-- we had to watch the way they kill his brother, who was the lawyer? He was the doctor. And I told him what happened to his brother, and he told me what happened to my-- our mother. So that's the way I found out. They made them a memorial thing there in the cemetery in [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my mom, Gorgette. This is my brother Saul, his lovely wife Hope, and their three beautiful children Alex, Jonathan, and Samantha. This is my loving husband, my daughter [? Allison, ?] and Rebecca. And I'm Jackie. This is my dear dad that I love very much, and I'm very grateful that we have this opportunity to have his incredible life story on tape. I am forever happy that we have him around. I love him very much, and I will cherish this for the rest of my life. I know this was very difficult for him. And this means a lot to all of us, for-- for his children and for his grandchildren. And that's it. We love you very much.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mr. Rubin?

speaker: interviewee
text: As you all know, we never had the opportunity or the pleasure of meeting my grandparents. Let them rest in peace. And my mother wrote a little letter to her back in September of 1977 that I'd like to share with you. It goes, 'A letter to momma, Regina Rubin. Sad summer afternoon when we went to visit you. We didn't know exactly where to find you, but we went very close to you. Lonely, sad grave with no name that we stood, the four of us-- your son [? Hasum, ?] me, his wife, and your two grandchildren. We all pray [? HaShem ?] for you, your soul. I, for myself, spoke to you, and in all that tranquility, I had a very strange sensation, like you and I were there just alone. Later, we went to Somotor, and I was so happy to see your house and the places where [? Hasum ?] had spent some years of his youth.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mama dear, people there still mention your name with great dignity. You must have been a very kind and fine lady. Why are you so close to me and so often in my thoughts? I really don't know, and I'll always be. Mama, I do love you. You mean so much to me. I'll try to go back to be near you again. If I'm 1,000 miles away from your resting place, my thoughts-- by thoughts you aren't out of reach.

speaker: interviewee
text: Dear Mama, I wonder very often if you will had been happy to have me as your daughter-in-law. However, Mama, remember that [? Hasum ?] means the world to me. And if you didn't want to be my mama, I will have settled to be just your friend. Shalom, Gorgette [? Esta ?] Rubin.'
